subject: Platinum
executive_summary:
Platinum faces a structural supply deficit that has persisted for three consecutive years (2023-2025) and is projected to continue through at least 2029. The World Platinum Investment Council (WPIC) estimates the 2025 deficit at 692,000-850,000 ounces, representing nearly 9% of annual demand. Above-ground stocks have declined to approximately five months of global consumption, the lowest since 2020. South Africa dominates global mine supply at over 70%, with Russia contributing another significant share — meaning the two most geopolitically complex platinum sources account for the vast majority of world output.

The United States is heavily import-dependent for platinum group metals (PGMs), with domestic mine production limited to one company in Montana producing approximately 2,000 kilograms annually against U.S. consumption of 71,000 kilograms in 2024. Import sources for platinum (2020-23) were: South Africa 45%, Belgium 12%, Germany 10%, Italy 9%, and other 24%. For palladium, Russia and South Africa each supplied 32% of U.S. imports. The estimated value of domestic PGM production was approximately $310 million in 2024, a 42% decrease from $541 million in 2023, reflecting declining precious metal prices.

Platinum's strategic importance extends across multiple sectors: automotive catalytic converters remain the largest demand category, while the hydrogen economy represents the most significant growth vector, with WPIC projecting hydrogen-related applications to reach 11% of total annual platinum demand (~875,000 ounces) by 2030. Platinum serves as the critical catalyst in proton exchange membrane (PEM) fuel cells and electrolyzers essential to hydrogen infrastructure. Defense applications include catalytic systems, electronics, and specialized alloys. The combination of persistent supply deficits, extreme geographic concentration in South Africa and Russia, growing hydrogen economy demand, and limited substitution options creates a compounding risk profile.

key_findings.headline: Third consecutive year of platinum supply deficit in 2025, projected at 692,000-850,000 ounces
key_findings.detail: The platinum market experienced its third consecutive annual supply deficit in 2025, estimated at 692,000 to 850,000 ounces — nearly 9% of total annual demand. The deficit is driven by constrained mine supply (South African production declining due to deep-level mining costs, electricity shortages, and labor disputes) against sustained demand from automotive catalysts and growing industrial applications. Above-ground stocks declined to approximately five months of global consumption, the lowest since 2020.

key_findings.headline: South Africa produces 70%+ of global platinum supply, creating extreme concentration risk
key_findings.detail: South Africa accounts for over 70% of global platinum mine supply, with Russia providing another significant share. South African production has been declining due to a combination of factors: increasing costs associated with deeper mining operations, persistent electricity supply constraints from Eskom, labor disputes, and planned metallurgical plant outages. These structural challenges constrain the supply response even as prices rise, creating a persistent deficit condition.

key_findings.headline: U.S. domestic PGM production limited to one Montana mine producing ~2,000 kg annually
key_findings.detail: One company in Montana (Sibanye-Stillwater) mined and processed PGMs with an estimated value of about $310 million in 2024, a 42% decrease from $541 million in 2023. Small additional quantities were recovered as byproducts of copper-nickel mining in Michigan but were sold to foreign companies for refining. U.S. consumption of PGMs totaled approximately 71,000 kilograms of platinum in 2024, supported primarily by imports of 72,000 kilograms — domestic production covers less than 3% of consumption.

key_findings.headline: Hydrogen economy projected to drive 11% of platinum demand by 2030
key_findings.detail: The WPIC projects that hydrogen-related end uses — primarily PEM fuel cells and electrolyzers — will account for 11% of total annual platinum demand, approximately 875,000 ounces, by 2030. Platinum serves as the critical catalyst in proton exchange membrane technologies used for both hydrogen production (electrolyzers) and hydrogen consumption (fuel cells). This emerging demand vector could significantly widen the existing supply deficit if mine production does not increase proportionally.

key_findings.headline: Russia supplies 32% of U.S. palladium imports alongside geopolitical risk
key_findings.detail: Russia and South Africa each supplied 32% of U.S. palladium imports during 2020-23, while Russia continues to be a significant PGM producer globally. The ongoing Russia-Ukraine conflict creates geopolitical risk for Russian PGM supply flows, though direct sanctions on PGMs have been limited. Any expansion of sanctions or Russian retaliatory export restrictions would immediately impact U.S. palladium availability and could affect platinum markets through substitution dynamics.

key_findings.headline: Supply deficit projected to persist through 2029, narrowing only gradually
key_findings.detail: The WPIC projects the platinum supply deficit to persist through at least 2029, narrowing from an estimated 850,000 ounces in 2025 to approximately 486,000 ounces as recycling activity increases. However, the narrowing assumes no major supply disruptions and only moderate demand growth from hydrogen applications. Structural underinvestment in new South African mine capacity means the supply constraint is deeply embedded and cannot be quickly resolved.

recent_developments.headline: Platinum prices surge to historic highs amid third consecutive supply deficit
recent_developments.summary: Platinum prices experienced a historic surge in 2025, driven by the third consecutive annual supply deficit estimated at 692,000-850,000 ounces. New futures markets were introduced to meet growing investor interest. Above-ground stocks declined to approximately five months of consumption, and the WPIC projected continued deficits through 2029, creating a structural bullish case for platinum prices in the medium term.
recent_developments.impact: Rising prices improve the economics of existing PGM mining operations and recycling but have not yet stimulated significant new mine development due to long lead times and the structural challenges facing South African mining. Higher platinum costs flow through to automotive catalytic converters, fuel cell systems, and chemical process equipment.

recent_developments.headline: WPIC updates five-year supply and demand outlook projecting persistent deficits
recent_developments.summary: The World Platinum Investment Council published an updated two-to-five-year supply and demand outlook in September 2025, projecting annual deficits persisting through 2029. The outlook incorporated growing hydrogen economy demand (targeting 11% of total demand, ~875,000 ounces, by 2030) alongside constrained South African mine supply. Recycling is expected to provide partial relief, narrowing deficits from 850,000 ounces in 2025 to 486,000 ounces by 2029.
recent_developments.impact: The multi-year deficit projection signals that platinum supply constraints are structural rather than cyclical. Defense, industrial, and automotive consumers face sustained cost pressure and potential allocation challenges. The hydrogen economy demand growth could accelerate deficits if deployments outpace recycling capacity development.

recent_developments.headline: Sibanye-Stillwater Montana PGM production value drops 42% to $310 million
recent_developments.summary: The only U.S. PGM mine producer, Sibanye-Stillwater's Montana operations, reported an estimated production value of approximately $310 million in 2024 — a 42% decline from $541 million in 2023. The decrease was driven by lower precious metal prices (rhodium down 31%, palladium down 27%, platinum slightly lower) and reflected broader PGM market weakness before the 2025 price recovery. This single-facility domestic production base remains extremely small relative to U.S. consumption.
recent_developments.impact: Declining U.S. production value underscores the fragility of the domestic PGM supply base. A single-operator, single-mine production structure means any operational disruption would eliminate virtually all domestic PGM production. The production value decline may affect future investment in expanding or maintaining Montana operations.

risk_assessment.current_risk: Platinum supply risk is elevated with structural deficits persisting for three consecutive years and projected through 2029. South Africa's 70%+ share of global supply is constrained by electricity shortages, deep-mining costs, and labor instability. Russia's significant role (32% of U.S. palladium imports) adds geopolitical risk. The U.S. produces less than 3% of its PGM consumption domestically from a single mine in Montana. Growing hydrogen economy demand could widen deficits further. Above-ground stocks at five-month lows provide minimal buffer.
key_drivers: Third consecutive annual supply deficit (692,000-850,000 oz in 2025), projected through 2029
South Africa produces 70%+ of global supply with declining output from structural challenges
U.S. domestic production covers less than 3% of consumption from one Montana mine
Russia supplies 32% of U.S. palladium imports amid geopolitical uncertainty
Hydrogen economy demand projected to reach 11% of total platinum demand by 2030
Above-ground stocks at approximately five months of consumption, lowest since 2020
mitigation_actions: Invest in expanding domestic PGM recycling capacity from automotive catalytic converters
Support Sibanye-Stillwater Montana operations and evaluate potential capacity expansion
Establish strategic platinum stockpile for defense and critical industrial applications
Develop bilateral PGM supply agreements with South Africa and Canada
Fund R&D on platinum thrifting technologies for fuel cells and catalysts
Diversify PGM sourcing through partnerships with Zimbabwe, Canada, and Finland
